paragraphs.body: To be brave, or not to be What if you were lost, never to be found again in the deep foliage of the woods? You push past the warnings of “Don’t go there if you know what’s best for you!”, and “You don’t know what you’re doing”, and keep rustling through the bushes. After brushing aside leaves and stray branches, the hint of a sandy path reaches your eyes. Turns out the only things that had been holding you back were your own thoughts, and misguided fear. That extra push was all you needed. Growing up, I would often become annoyed at my mother always trying to protect me, although I knew deep down, she only meant the best for me.

annotations.comment: Great voice and tone in this section!
annotations.context_left: t my mother always trying to protect me, although I knew deep down, she only meant the best for me.

paragraphs.body: While searching for a topic at hand, I happened to come across a YouTube video titled, “The Horrors of Becoming Lost”, from a documentary channel called Abstract. In the 48-minute-long video, the narrator analyzes through 3 harrowing stories of groups and individuals making different mistakes, which equally led to their demise in nature. Different situations, led to the individuals' making choices that led to their death. With all of this to process, one can’t help but wonder why bother exploring in the first place? As the overall video mentions, following exact instructions is safe, and generally approved by society. But what if you ignored the right directions, and pushed yourself to your own limits? Is bravery so bad?

I know my mother is for sure one of these people that question exploration. Every time I set off on an excursion her words always linger in my mind- “Why are you going out there all by yourself”, and “I’d rather you just stay on campus!” My mother acts so fearful for my actions, yet she doesn’t realize- or see, how brave she is herself. I can’t help but trail my mind back to our little adventure we had when she visited me in Pennsylvania, for my birthday. We visited a park, a beautiful little spot right next to a vibrant, sprawling river. Before the river, a large hill of grass grew. Sharp green blades sloped downwards touching the riverbank. Beyond the river one could peek over and see a lush forest in the background. My mom climbed up the stairs leading to the hill, and looked back at me- giving me a simple head turn to follow her steps. Following her que, I stalked behind my mother, watching as she lowered herself onto the glass, sliding it expertly! When she hit the bottom of the grassy hillside, she stared up at me- again, giving me that knowing nod to come down. I simply stared in awe. The hill loomed, seemingly towered over everything, appearing so threatening. How was she not scared?

paragraphs.body: I always knew about the strength my mother holds. I admire and look up to the hardened steel that makes up my mother. Her body sheltered me from my father’s blows, his insults, his violent bursting anger. She alone protected me, took the hits, and raised me all throughout this. She had no idea if she would live the next day, but continued going, all for me, in hopes of a day where I could understand all her sacrifices. That’s why she wasn’t scared of a hill, she had already crossed her own large hill years ago, with a tiny child at that. She always trusted her gut, and from that day onwards- after I slid down the hill after her, I started to trust mine as well.

annotations.text: continued going, all for me, in hopes of a day where I could understand all her sacrifices. That’s why she wasn’t scared of a hill, she had already crossed her own large hill years ago, with a tiny child at that.

paragraphs.body: My gut, listen to my gut. My mom always says this whenever I bring up something that I’m questioning about. When I was younger, I would be annoyed with her saying this, thinking she would just push my feelings aside, but as I grew older, I began to hold this advice dear to my heart. My mom always warns me, and gets nervous whenever I have a urge to go hiking, or caving. My pride would always get in the way, as I’m always the one to reassure her that “I’ve got this”, or “I’ll be alright.” Much before I watched this video, I didn’t really take accord of to most of the danger that lurked within doing these hobbies. Much less, that the very muddy footprints I left in the path could be the last piece of evidence to my very own disappearance.

annotations.text: footprints I left in the path could be the last piece of evidence to my very own disappearance.

paragraphs.body: (What if these tracks left were the last known traces of me and Julia due to a wrong turn or ignoring directions?)

I love my mother so much, and I appreciate the adventures we went on together. I would often ask her questions about her life, and herself at that. “Mama, if you never met dad, what would you do?” I asked my mom about this one tender day, in the quietlike childlike wonder kids normally have when asking invasive questions. My mom later replied, “I can’t imagine a life without you in it.” This moment stays in my mind, even now, as even through she experienced so many negative and violent things from meeting my dad, she would do it all over again to have me. That, is true bravery I look up to. Even though my mom worries about me going off on my own adventures, it comes from a place where she herself once was worried for herself, and her wellbeing. Even if the day ended in violence, I still appreciate the shared mornings, sleepy as we eat breakfast together against the wooden table, and the afternoons we have lounging with our cats and watching tv. Just because you’re brave doesn’t mean you want to be. Given the circumstances, I would give anything to feel safe — free from looming threats and fear of the world. Unfortunately, we don't get to choose the cards we're dealt; we just have to face what comes. No matter what, there will always be a light at the end of the tunnel, if you push yourself in the right way forwards.

imagine a life without you in it.”

This essay is a beauty! Wow. Well done. You develop a powerful, surprising, and sophisticated metaphor and connection between exploration, bravery, and your mother's example of both things. Really well done. I hope you share this one with her :)
